a:r.a:t: The graph module supports 4 query methods, each optimized for different types of questions. Local queries traverse entity relationships within document clusters - great for "what entities connect contract risk to vendor performance?" type questions. This requires 5 graph artifacts to be built. Global queries generate corpus-wide summaries and cross-document themes - useful for "what are the main risk categories across all our contracts?" This requires 3 artifacts. Drift queries track temporal changes and evolution across graph rebuilds - so you can see how entity relationships changed between two versions of a document corpus. This also requires 5 artifacts. SQL queries go through the StructuredSearchAdapter for exact matches, metadata filtering, and structured queries that do not need semantic search. The build pipeline runs in three phases. Phase 1 is Entity and Relationship Extraction via LLM - the system reads documents and uses the LLM to identify named entities (people, organizations, concepts) and the relationships between them. Phase 2 is Community Detection - the system groups related entities into communities and generates summary reports. This uses native graph algorithms with a text-based fallback. Phase 3 is Embedding and Indexing - entity descriptions and community reports get vectorized for semantic search. Two backends are available: MicrosoftGraphRagBackend provides the full local/global/drift query pipeline, and Neo4jGraphImportBackend enables importing existing Neo4j graph databases.
